title: Volatility vs. permanent loss of capital
subtitle: These are not the same risk. Confusing them causes investors to make exactly the wrong decisions at exactly the wrong moments.

body.content: When markets fall, most people feel they are losing money. In many cases, they are not. They are experiencing volatility — a temporary reduction in market price that, if they do nothing, will likely recover. Permanent loss of capital is a different phenomenon, and it requires different conditions to occur.

body.label: Critical distinction
body.content: Volatility is a paper loss that reverses over time. Permanent capital loss is realized — either by selling during a downturn, or by holding an asset that genuinely fails to recover (a bankrupt company, a structurally declining industry). The mechanism of loss is entirely different.

Volatility only becomes permanent loss when you sell. This is why investor behavior during market downturns is so consequential. An investor who holds a diversified portfolio through a 40% decline and continues holding will, based on all historical data, recover and grow. An investor who sells at the bottom locks in that loss permanently.

The second mechanism is concentration. A diversified portfolio experiencing a 30% drop is experiencing market volatility. A concentrated position in a single company that drops 80% may be experiencing genuine structural deterioration — a very different situation where the expected recovery does not exist.

items: Volatility: temporary price decline in a fundamentally sound asset. Time is the remedy.
Permanent loss via behavior: selling a volatile position before recovery. Avoidance is the remedy.
Permanent loss via selection: holding a failing asset. Research and diversification are the remedies.

body.content: When a market falls, ask: is this volatility or genuine deterioration? The answer determines whether the correct response is to hold, buy more, or exit. Panic is never the correct response to either.
